planting.note: Plant in spring; foliage stays semi-evergreen in mild climates.

directions: Plant the crown level with the soil in a sunny, well-drained spot. In warmer zones the fine green foliage stays largely evergreen through winter, unlike most maiden grasses. Tall, slender flower stalks carry coppery plumes that appear over a long season from summer into fall; groom out old foliage in early spring.

planting.note: Transplant in spring for this tall white-variegated selection.

directions: Plant the crown flush with the grade in full sun and keep moist until rooted. Its tall blades are edged in bright white, giving a luminous vertical accent to large borders. Silvery-pink plumes rise above the foliage in late summer and hold their color into winter.

planting.note: Sow or plant in spring once soil has warmed.

directions: Sow seed shallowly on warm soil or set transplants at their original depth in full sun. This vigorous upland switchgrass forms a sturdy, upright clump and tolerates heat, drought, and poor soils once established. Airy seed panicles open in late summer and the foliage turns golden in fall; leave standing for winter and cut back in early spring.

planting.note: Direct sow or transplant in spring after frost.

directions: Plant in full sun in average to lean soil; press seed lightly into warm ground rather than burying it deep. This upright native selection is tough and adaptable, handling drought and occasional wet spells. Fine flower panicles rise in summer and foliage colors to yellow-orange in fall.

planting.note: Sow or plant in spring on warmed soil.

directions: Set plants at soil level or scatter seed on a firm, warm seedbed in full sun. A broad-leaved, upland switchgrass valued for reliable clumps and good drought tolerance. Delicate panicles bloom in late summer and the blue-green blades shift to warm yellow in autumn; leave the clump for winter structure.

planting.note: Plant in spring; this lowland type tolerates wet ground and rain gardens.

directions: Plant in full sun in average to moist soil, setting the crown level with the surface. This tall lowland switchgrass handles periodic flooding and heavy clay, making it useful along ponds and in rain gardens. Feathery panicles top the strong stems in late summer and the whole clump stands well into winter.

planting.note: Sow or plant in late spring; this southern lowland type favors warmth.

directions: Choose a warm, sunny site and press seed into a firm bed or plant transplants at grade. A tall, robust lowland switchgrass that thrives in southern heat and tolerates occasional wet soil. Large open panicles appear from midsummer, and the vigorous upright clump provides bold vertical structure through the cold months.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring for reliable red fall color.

directions: Set the division at its original depth in full sun and average, well-drained soil. This selection is grown for deep wine-red foliage tips that intensify through summer into a strong burgundy fall display. Airy reddish panicles hover above the clump in late summer; cut the plant back to a few inches in early spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring; foliage flushes red as the season progresses.

directions: Plant the crown level with the soil in full sun and water until established. Blue-green blades take on rich red tones by late summer, deepening to burgundy in fall. Clouds of fine reddish seed heads float above the upright clump; leave it standing through winter and shear back before new growth in spring.

planting.note: Transplant in spring for early, dark-flowering plumes.

directions: Set the division level with the soil in a sunny, well-drained bed and keep evenly moist until established. Grown for dark, blackish-purple bottlebrush flowers that appear earlier than most fountain grasses above lush green foliage. Leave the seed heads standing for fall and winter and cut the clump back before spring growth.

planting.note: Plant in spring; blooms over a long summer season.

directions: Plant the crown at grade in full sun and well-drained soil, watering through the first season. This oriental fountain grass produces long, creamy-white foxtail plumes on tall arching stems over a lengthy bloom period. Enjoy the soft flowers from early summer into fall, then cut the clump back in early spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring for a strong fall flowering display.

directions: Set the crown level with the soil in full sun and water in well. This compact fountain grass flowers freely, carrying silvery bottlebrush plumes above a tidy mound of green foliage. The plumes appear from late summer into fall and fade to tan; cut the clump back in early spring before new shoots emerge.

planting.note: Plant in spring; this taller form blooms later in the season.

directions: Plant the crown at grade in full sun and well-drained soil, keeping moist while it establishes. A taller, later-flowering fountain grass with narrow foliage and elegant arching form. Long bottlebrush plumes appear in late summer and early fall; leave them for winter texture and shear the clump low in early spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring for an upright blue clump with vivid fall tones.

directions: Plant the crown at grade in full sun and well-drained soil. Bred for tall, sturdy, upright stems and steel-blue summer foliage that turns mahogany-red and purple in fall. It stays reliably vertical in lean conditions; leave it for winter structure and cut back in early spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring; hardier than most muhly grasses for northern gardens.

directions: Plant the crown level with the soil in full sun and well-drained ground; it excels in heat and drought and is cold-hardier than pink muhly. Fine green foliage forms a soft mound through summer. In fall it is topped by a cloud of ruby-pink flower panicles; leave for winter interest and cut back in early spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring for a dependable deep-pink fall show.

directions: Plant the crown at grade in full sun and fast-draining soil, watering only until established. This selection of pink muhly is noted for its rich, deep-pink to red autumn plumes and uniform habit. Fine green foliage vanishes under the floral haze each fall; leave the display through winter and cut back in early spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a robust muhly with showy late-season plumes.

directions: Set the crown level with the soil in full sun and well-drained ground; it withstands heat and drought once established. The plant forms a lush mound of broad green foliage larger and fuller than most muhly grasses. Airy pinkish to purple flower panicles rise above the clump in fall, aging to silvery tan; cut back in late winter.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a shaded rock garden or trough with sharp drainage and steady moisture.

directions: This compact selection stays only a few inches tall, making it ideal for troughs and shaded crevices. Give it gritty, humus-rich soil that drains freely yet never fully dries, and shelter from midday sun. Remove old fronds in late winter before the tiny fiddleheads appear.

planting.note: Grow in a frost-free greenhouse or as a large houseplant; only summer outdoors in shade.

directions: This large tropical maidenhair bears broad diamond-shaped pinnae on arching fronds and needs warmth, shade, and constant humidity. Pot in a rich, moisture-retentive mix and never let it dry out, which quickly scorches the foliage. Feed lightly through the growing season and repot each spring as it enlarges.

planting.note: Grow as a warm indoor or greenhouse fern; keep away from all frost.

directions: Prized for its unusually large coin-sized pinnae that emerge a pretty coppery-pink, this fern demands warmth, shade, and unfailing moisture. Grow in a rich, open mix, keep humidity high, and shield from cold drafts. Trim spent fronds at the base to encourage a steady flush of new growth.

planting.note: Keep as a frost-free container plant in shade with high humidity.

directions: Grow this tender fern in a rich, moisture-retentive mix in warm shade, giving it the humidity of a bathroom or greenhouse. Its stalks snap easily, so handle carefully and water before the medium dries. Cut away any browned fronds to keep the plant looking fresh.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sheltered shaded bed; it is hardier than most maidenhairs.

directions: This tough garden hybrid handles more cold than its tender parents, making it a reliable maidenhair for sheltered borders. Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in shade and mulch the crown for winter protection. Keep it evenly watered and cut back old fronds in early spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a frost-sheltered, shaded, moist woodland spot.

directions: One of the taller maidenhairs, it sends up large lacy fronds on dark, near-black stems from creeping rhizomes. Give it deep, moist, humus-rich soil in sheltered shade and protect the crown from hard frost. Water generously in summer and allow it room to spread.

planting.note: Grow as a frost-free houseplant or greenhouse fern in bright shade.

directions: A robust, full-fronded selection of delta maidenhair valued as an easy indoor fern with dense overlapping foliage. Pot in an airy, moisture-retentive mix, keep humidity high, and water before the surface dries. Divide in spring when the pot fills and trim any browned fronds at the base.

planting.note: Grow in a frost-free hanging basket in warm shade with high humidity.

directions: The long arching fronds root at their tips and form new plantlets, so this fern is best displayed in a hanging basket. Keep the mix moist and the air humid, and pin down frond tips into pots to propagate. Guard against cold drafts and drying, which brown the delicate foliage.

planting.note: Plant in fall to establish with winter rains in shaded, well-drained slopes.

directions: This West Coast native grows actively in the cool, moist months and naturally goes dormant through summer drought. Plant on a shaded, well-drained bank with fast-draining soil and water during the growing season only. Do not overwater the dormant crown in summer, and enjoy the fresh fronds after fall rains.

planting.note: Grow as a frost-free container plant in warm, humid shade.

directions: Grown for its bold, unusually large pinnae that flush a striking rose-red when young, this tropical maidenhair needs warmth and constant moisture. Pot in a rich, open mix, keep humidity high, and never let the roots dry. Site out of direct sun and cold drafts to preserve the colorful new growth.

planting.note: Plant in spring into limestone crevices or old mortar joints in light shade.

directions: This tiny lime-loving fern naturally colonizes mortared walls, so give it an alkaline crevice with sharp drainage and only modest moisture. Wedge the small crown into a gap packed with gritty, limy soil and let it establish slowly. It needs almost no attention once rooted but hates rich, wet ground.

planting.note: Grow in a frost-free shaded spot or container; move outdoors only when nights are mild.

directions: This graceful fern produces tiny plantlets along its fronds that can be pegged down to root and form new plants. Grow in rich, moist, well-drained soil in shade with high humidity, protecting it from frost. Keep evenly watered and detach the little bulbils once they have roots to propagate.

planting.note: Plant in spring into a cool, moist, limy rock crevice in light shade.

directions: Similar to maidenhair spleenwort but with a green rather than black rachis, this alpine fern favors cool, moist limestone crevices. Tuck the crown into gritty, lime-rich soil with good drainage and steady but gentle moisture. It suits shaded troughs and rock gardens in cooler climates.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a shaded, well-drained wall or rock crevice.

directions: This evergreen spleenwort bears leathery, glossy dark-green triangular fronds on near-black stalks and thrives in wall and rock crevices. Give it gritty, well-drained soil in part shade and avoid waterlogging the roots. It is drought-tolerant once established and needs little upkeep.

planting.note: Plant in spring into a sunny to lightly shaded limy crevice with sharp drainage.

directions: Named for the rusty scales that coat the undersides of its fronds, this drought-hardy fern curls up when dry and revives after rain. Plant in a gritty, alkaline crevice with excellent drainage in sun or light shade. It tolerates dry spells better than most ferns, so avoid rich, wet soil.

planting.note: Grow as a frost-free container or houseplant in bright shade with high humidity.

directions: This rosette-forming epiphytic fern holds broad, glossy, undivided fronds around a central nest-like crown. Pot in a coarse, free-draining organic mix, water into the soil rather than the crown, and keep humidity high in bright indirect light. Wipe dust from the leaves and avoid cold drafts.

planting.note: Plant in spring in cool, shaded, limestone-rich soil with steady moisture.

directions: The rare North American form of hart's tongue clings to cool, shaded limestone in deep woods and grows slowly. Give it a shaded, moist, lime-rich site with excellent drainage and stable cool temperatures. It resents heat and disturbance, so choose the spot carefully and leave it undisturbed.

planting.note: Plant in spring into shaded, limy rock crevices with sharp drainage.

directions: This natural hybrid between ebony spleenwort and walking fern shows irregular, often tail-tipped fronds that fascinate collectors. Grow it in a shaded, gritty, lime-rich crevice with good drainage and gentle moisture. It appreciates the cool, rocky conditions of its wild haunts and little else.

planting.note: Plant in spring in cool, shaded, humus-rich woodland soil.

directions: This distinctive Asian woodland fern forms fronds shaped like a three-pronged spear, with an extended tip and a pair of side branches near the base. Plant in moist, well-drained, humus-rich soil in shade and keep it evenly watered. Cut back old fronds in late winter before the new growth pushes up.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a shaded rock garden or crevice with limy, well-drained soil.

directions: A small evergreen holly fern whose narrow fronds root at their tips to form new plantlets across shaded rock. Give it gritty, lime-rich, well-drained soil in shade with steady but modest moisture. Let the arching tips touch soil to walk across a crevice and increase the colony.

planting.note: Plant in spring in cool, shaded, humus-rich, well-drained soil.

directions: This vigorous garden hybrid carries large, soft, finely divided evergreen fronds and often forms plantlets along the midrib. Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in shade and mulch the crown. Peg down bulbil-bearing fronds to propagate and tidy old foliage in early spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sheltered, shaded, moist but well-drained spot.

directions: A handsome evergreen shield fern with leathery dark-green fronds and shaggy scaly stalks, forming a bold rosette. Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in shade or part shade with shelter from cold drying winds. Keep it watered through dry spells and remove weathered fronds in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a cool, shaded, gritty rock crevice with sharp drainage.

directions: This small cold-hardy fern bears aromatic evergreen fronds and clings to rocky ledges in the far north. Give it a cool, gritty, well-drained crevice in part shade and avoid rich, soggy soil. The persistent old fronds ring the crown, so leave them or trim lightly in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in cool, shaded, humus-rich, well-drained woodland soil.

directions: This tidy Asian wood fern holds finely textured, semi-evergreen fronds that stay presentable well into winter. Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in shade and keep it evenly watered while establishing. Remove tattered fronds in early spring before the new croziers unfurl.

planting.note: Plant in spring in cool, shaded, moist, humus-rich soil.

directions: A robust semi-evergreen fern of hybrid origin, it forms a full shuttlecock of arching, softly divided fronds. Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in shade and mulch to keep the roots cool. It is trouble-free and holds its foliage late in the season.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, shaded, humus-rich soil, tolerating damp ground.

directions: This natural hybrid of crested and intermediate wood ferns makes tall, upright semi-evergreen fronds and tolerates wet woodland soils. Plant in moist to boggy humus-rich ground in shade and keep the roots damp. It needs little care beyond removing old fronds in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, shaded, humus-rich, well-drained soil.

directions: A bold, vigorous relative of the male fern, it forms a large upright vase of leathery fronds and takes both shade and some morning sun. Plant in deep, moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil and water through dry spells while young. It is heat-tolerant for a wood fern and largely evergreen in mild zones.

planting.note: Plant in spring in cool, shaded, humus-rich, well-drained soil.

directions: This Asian wood fern shows dark, finely cut, semi-evergreen fronds with elongated drooping tips that give it an elegant look. Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in shade and keep evenly watered while establishing. Cut back weathered fronds in late winter before new growth appears.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, shaded, humus-rich woodland soil.

directions: Formerly grouped with the lady ferns, this native deciduous fern is named for the silvery sheen of its developing spore cases beneath the fronds. Plant in consistently moist, humus-rich soil in shade and shelter it from drying wind. Keep the soil damp all season and cut back the foliage after frost.

planting.note: Plant in spring in rich, moist, shaded woodland soil where it can colonize.

directions: This graceful native fern, once placed with the lady ferns, bears once-divided fronds with distinctly narrow, entire leaflets that give an airy look. Plant in deep, moist, humus-rich soil in shade and let its creeping habit form a soft colony over time. Keep the soil evenly moist and cut back spent fronds after frost.

planting.note: Divide or plant in spring so clumps settle before summer bloom.

directions: Set divisions or nursery plants at their original depth in average, moisture-retentive soil and full sun. This dependable clump-former blankets itself in golden-orange, black-centered daisies from midsummer into autumn. Shear back after flowering or leave seed heads for winter interest and birds.

planting.note: Dormant-sow in autumn for the cold-moist conditioning seed requires.

directions: Surface-sow into sharply drained sandy soil in fall and avoid rich, wet ground that rots the crown. Bold blue-lavender trumpets, the largest of any native penstemon, encircle the stems in late spring above waxy blue leaves. Give it lean, dry, sunny sites and let it reseed, as individual plants are short-lived.

planting.note: Set out plants or divisions in spring; seed is slow and needs stratification.

directions: Plant nursery stock or divisions in spring at the original depth, as this deep-rooted species resents disturbance later. Fine, thread-like foliage forms a soft mound topped by pale blue star flowers in late spring. Its real glory comes in fall when the whole plant turns brilliant gold, so give it full sun and room.

planting.note: Sow in early spring; light cold-moist treatment improves germination.

directions: Sow shallowly in early spring into average soil, where this adaptable native blooms readily. Golden-yellow daisy flowers with slightly raised centers cover the plants from early summer into fall. Deadhead to prolong bloom, and enjoy how it thrives in tougher soils than true sunflowers.

planting.note: Fall-sow the hard seed for winter stratification; taproot resists moving.

directions: Sow in fall directly where wanted, since the deep taproot makes established plants hard to transplant. Trailing stems form a low mat studded with cup-shaped magenta flowers from late spring through summer. Exceptionally drought-tolerant, it sprawls beautifully over walls, slopes, and dry, sunny beds.

planting.note: Set out in autumn so winter rains establish roots before summer heat.

directions: Plant this fast-growing large shrub or small tree at the same depth it sat in the nursery pot, leaving the crown slightly high to shed water. Water occasionally the first summer, then withhold irrigation once established since it resents summer moisture at the root crown. Clouds of medium-blue flower clusters open in early spring and draw bees and butterflies.

planting.note: Autumn planting lets roots settle in before the first dry season.

directions: Dig a hole no deeper than the rootball and backfill with native, unamended soil for this arching evergreen shrub. Deep, cobalt-blue flower clusters emerge from reddish buds in mid-spring, and the plant needs no summer water once established. Prune only lightly after bloom to keep the graceful form.

planting.note: Plant in fall on slopes where it can spread and control erosion.

directions: Space plants widely on a bank or open bed and set the crown slightly proud of the soil line. This low, wide-spreading evergreen carries glossy dark leaves and medium-blue spring flowers that hum with pollinators. Give occasional deep soaks the first year, then leave it dry through summer.

planting.note: Plant in fall in a well-drained spot with light afternoon shade inland.

directions: Set this evergreen subshrub in fast-draining soil and avoid overhead watering that can rot the sticky foliage. Apricot to orange trumpet flowers cover the plant from spring into summer and are a hummingbird favorite. Shear back after the main flush to tidy the form and encourage rebloom.

planting.note: Plant in fall on a dry slope with excellent drainage.

directions: Plant this vigorous evergreen high and dry and never water at the crown, as summer moisture is quickly fatal. Waxy golden-yellow saucer flowers smother the branches in spring and continue sporadically after. Wear gloves and long sleeves when pruning because the fuzzy leaf hairs irritate skin.

planting.note: Plant in fall in dappled shade with good drainage.

directions: Tuck this evergreen mounding perennial into part shade with the crown just at soil level to prevent rot. Tall airy stems of tiny pink-white bells rise above bold rounded leaves from late winter into spring. Divide crowded clumps every few years and refresh mulch to keep the foliage lush.

planting.note: Plant in fall in a spot that stays moist in winter and dries in summer.

directions: Set these small grassy clumps at soil level in a bed that receives winter and spring moisture. Star-shaped blue-purple flowers with yellow throats open in the morning sun from late winter into spring. Plants go dormant and disappear in summer drought, then reappear with fall rains.

planting.note: Plant in spring in hot, fast-draining desert soil.

directions: Set this gray-leaved desert subshrub in sharply drained soil and water sparingly once roots take hold. Cupped apricot-orange flowers cover the wands through spring and rebloom after summer rain. Shear the plant back hard after the first flush to keep it dense and encourage another round.

planting.note: Plant in fall in coastal-influenced, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant this rounded evergreen shrub in lean soil where it needs no supplemental summer water. Bright yellow daisies with dark centers cover the plant from late winter through spring on long stems. Cut back leggy growth after bloom to renew the mounded shape.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun and sharply drained desert soil.

directions: Give this silvery desert shrub full sun and gritty soil, and avoid rich amendments or heavy water. Golden daisy flowers on long stalks float above the woolly foliage in spring, sometimes again after summer storms. It may drop leaves in extreme drought and leaf out again once rain returns.

planting.note: Sow directly in spring or fall in hot, well-drained sites.

directions: Scatter seed on open, gritty ground and press it in lightly for this short-lived, self-sowing perennial. Bright yellow daisies on long stems bloom nearly year-round in warm climates above woolly gray leaves. Let some flowers set seed so the colony renews itself naturally.

planting.note: Plant in fall in well-drained soil with lean fertility.

directions: Plant this aromatic mint relative in fast-draining soil and water only occasionally through summer. Rounded lavender flower heads bloom from late spring into summer and swarm with butterflies and bees. Shear spent blooms and rub the fragrant leaves to release their minty scent.

planting.note: Plant in fall on a dry, sharply drained slope.

directions: Plant this evergreen chaparral shrub high in gritty, unamended soil and never water the crown in summer. Woolly purple-blue flower spikes rise above narrow aromatic leaves from spring into summer, feeding hummingbirds. Excellent drainage is essential, so avoid clay and any overhead irrigation.

planting.note: Sow in fall in the desert garden for spring flowers.

directions: Scatter seed on open, sandy ground in fall and cover very lightly for this desert annual. Deep gentian-blue bells open in spring above rounded leaves and draw early bees. Some people find the foliage irritating to touch, so handle with care when thinning.

planting.note: Sow in fall or early spring in open, sunny ground.

directions: Sow seed directly onto loosened soil and cover lightly for this airy wildflower. Powder-blue pincushion flower heads dance on slender stems from late spring into summer and attract pollinators. Give occasional water in dry spells and let it self-sow through fallen seed.

planting.note: Plant in fall in part shade where it can go summer-dry.

directions: Plant this spiny, arching shrub at nursery depth in filtered light, wearing gloves to avoid the thorns. Pendant crimson fuchsia-like flowers line the branches in late winter and early spring for the hummingbirds. It drops leaves and rests in summer drought, so withhold water once established.

planting.note: Plant in fall high in well-drained soil and mulch with gravel.

directions: Set this reliable evergreen shrub slightly high and never bury the crown, which is prone to rot. Small pink-white urn flowers cover the plant in late winter above glossy leaves and smooth mahogany bark. It tolerates occasional summer water better than most manzanitas but still prefers a dry rest.

planting.note: Plant in fall in a spot with light afternoon shade inland.

directions: Plant this evergreen shrub at nursery depth in well-drained soil with some afternoon shade in hot regions. Fragrant white flowers with golden stamens, resembling anemones, open in late spring and early summer. Give occasional deep water while young, then taper off as it settles in.

planting.note: Plant in fall near a moist streambank-style setting.

directions: Plant this large deciduous shrub in soil that stays reliably moist, mimicking its native creek margins. Reddish-maroon flowers with a wine-and-spice fragrance appear from late spring through summer amid aromatic foliage. Keep it watered through the dry season and prune out old canes in winter to renew.

planting.note: Plant in fall in lean, fast-draining soil.

directions: Plant this mat-forming perennial in gritty soil and water only occasionally once it settles in. Golden-yellow daisies cover the silvery woolly foliage from late spring into summer and draw many pollinators. Shear off spent flowers to tidy the plant and encourage a fuller mound.

planting.note: Plant in fall in average soil with room to arch.

directions: Plant this deciduous shrub at nursery depth in average, well-drained soil and give it space to arch. Clusters of fragrant white flowers with a sweet citrus scent bloom in late spring and early summer. Prune the oldest canes right after flowering to keep the plant vigorous and open.

planting.note: Plant in fall in part shade with decent drainage.

directions: Plant this deciduous shrub at nursery depth in a spot with dappled light and average moisture. Foamy cascades of creamy white flowers drape the branches in early summer, aging to tan and persisting into fall. It tolerates a range of soils and needs little pruning beyond removing dead wood.

planting.note: Plant in fall in well-drained soil with light shade inland.

directions: Plant this sprawling evergreen subshrub in fast-draining soil and give it a bit of afternoon shade inland. Tubular red flowers appear over a long season from late winter into summer and feed hummingbirds. Cut it back hard in late winter to keep the plant dense and floriferous.

planting.note: Plant in fall or spring in well-drained soil.

directions: Plant this rounded evergreen perennial in fast-draining soil where it forms a lacy, ferny mound. Fragrant lilac-purple flower clusters bloom nearly year-round in mild climates and draw butterflies. Shear it lightly a couple times a year to keep it compact and flowering freely.

planting.note: Sow in fall or early spring in lean, well-drained soil.

directions: Sow seed directly onto loosened soil and barely cover it, as this taprooted perennial dislikes transplanting. Sky-blue five-petaled flowers open each morning on wiry stems from late spring into summer, dropping their petals by afternoon. It self-sows freely and needs little water once established.

planting.note: Sow seed in fall for natural cold stratification over winter.

directions: Sow seed directly in fall into deep, well-drained soil, since the long taproot resents disturbance. Large bright yellow sunflower-like blooms rise above silvery arrow-shaped leaves in late spring. It takes several years to reach flowering size but then persists for decades with no summer water.

planting.note: Surface-sow in light indoors and move seedlings out after frost.

directions: Press seed lightly into the surface and keep barely moist until sprouts appear. Grow in sandy, well-drained soil and full sun where it self-sows into loose colonies. Tiered whorls of spotted yellow flowers backed by showy pink bracts bloom in summer and swarm with wasps and native bees.

planting.note: Surface-sow indoors in light, then transplant into moist ground after frost.

directions: Start seed on the soil surface indoors and set plants in rich, consistently moist soil with room for air to circulate. Divide crowded clumps every few years to keep them vigorous and mildew-free. Shaggy scarlet flower heads bloom in summer and are irresistible to hummingbirds.

planting.note: Plant container-grown stock in spring; the taproot dislikes disturbance once settled.

directions: Plant in spring into full sun and well-drained soil, giving the wide clump room to fill in. It is drought-tolerant once rooted and needs no staking in an open site. Pale blue spring flowers give way to feathery green foliage that ignites into brilliant golden-orange in fall.

planting.note: This cultivar is grown from divisions or nursery plants set in spring.

directions: Plant this fine-textured selection in spring into full sun and average to moist soil. It is far more compact and drought-tolerant than common ironweeds and needs no staking. A haze of deep violet flowers covers the threadleaf mound in late summer to fall, feeding butterflies and bees.

planting.note: Start seed indoors or set divisions in spring into average soil.

directions: Start seed indoors or plant divisions in spring into full sun and average, well-drained soil. It tolerates heat, clay, and short droughts once rooted. Golden-yellow sunflower-like blooms cover the plant from early summer well into fall, especially if deadheaded, feeding bees and butterflies.

planting.note: Scarify and soak seed, then start warm indoors before setting out after frost.

directions: Nick and soak the hard seed, then start warm indoors and plant in full sun and moist to wet soil. It thrives at pond edges and in rain gardens and dies back to the ground each winter. Enormous crimson flowers up to six inches wide open across summer above deeply cut, maple-like leaves.

planting.note: One of the cold-hardiest agaves, it can be planted around the last frost.

directions: Plant rooted offsets in sharply drained, gravelly soil where winter wet cannot collect. The compact blue-green rosette shrugs off snow and hard freezes, making it a top choice for cold-climate rock gardens. Water deeply but rarely, and enjoy the golden flower spike that eventually rises after a decade or more.

planting.note: Give this large agave plenty of room and plant in spring after frost.

directions: Allow generous space for this fast, large agave whose smooth blue-green leaves have few marginal teeth. Detach the freely produced pups and plant in any well-drained soil in full sun. It is drought-tough and low-maintenance, forming a bold landscape mass before its single tall bloom.

planting.note: Plant offsets in spring after frost in a warm, sunny bed.

directions: Grow in gritty, well-drained soil in full sun to develop the chalky powder-blue leaves banded with cross markings. Remove and replant occasional offsets to expand a planting. Water lightly in summer and keep dry in winter, and admire the heavily toothed, sculptural rosette.

planting.note: Set out clumps in spring in a hot, well-drained spot.

directions: This unusual agave forms a spiky pincushion of stiff, cylindrical, needle-tipped leaves rather than a flat rosette. Plant divisions in sandy, fast-draining soil in full sun and water sparingly. It clumps slowly into a striking mound and, unlike most agaves, may survive its bloom.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sharply drained, sunny site.

directions: Long known as Agave ferdinandi-regis, this slow, solitary gem forms a compact dome of dark green leaves edged in white and tipped with black spines. Plant in a gritty mix in full sun and water very sparingly. Its geometric symmetry makes it a prized container and rock-garden specimen.

planting.note: Plant in spring in an open, sunny, fast-draining bed.

directions: Native to Arizona's central mountains, this agave forms a robust gray-green rosette and eventually a tall stalk crowded with bright yellow flowers. Plant in lean, gravelly soil in full sun and water only to establish. It handles cold better than many agaves and needs little care once settled.

planting.note: A cold-hardy selection that can be planted near the last frost date.

directions: This popular selection is prized for the toothy, interlocking leaf margins that give it its name, imprinted with bud markings. Plant offsets in well-drained soil in full sun to light shade in hot climates. It is notably cold-hardy and forms a bold, dramatic rosette with modest water.

planting.note: Plant after frost; protect from freezing temperatures.

directions: Grow this collector's agave in a very gritty, fast-draining mix, either in the ground in frost-free areas or in a pot to move indoors for winter. Full sun brings out the pale, chalky leaves and heavy, contrasting marginal teeth. Water sparingly and keep dry and warm in cold weather to avoid rot.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a hot, open, well-drained site.

directions: Plant in deep, sandy, fast-draining soil in full sun, where this trunk-forming yucca develops narrow, grass-like leaves with white curling filaments. Water occasionally the first year, then let it fend for itself in the desert. Tall stalks of creamy summer flowers rise above the crown once plants mature.

planting.note: Very cold-hardy; plant around the last frost in almost any soil.

directions: This nearly stemless yucca forms a rosette of stiff blue-green leaves fringed with curly white threads. Plant in well-drained soil in full sun; it is one of the most adaptable and cold-hardy yuccas. In early summer a tall spike carries dozens of nodding, creamy bell-shaped flowers.

planting.note: Plant in spring; tolerant of coastal and sandy soils.

directions: Plant this trunk-forming yucca in well-drained soil in full sun, where its stiff, sharply pointed blue-green leaves make a bold accent. It tolerates salt spray and sandy coastal ground as well as dry inland sites. Tall panicles of creamy white, sometimes purple-tinged flowers appear in late summer and fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in deep, extremely well-drained desert soil.

directions: This iconic Mojave tree-yucca needs deep, sharply drained soil, full sun, and minimal irrigation to grow slowly into its branching form. Water only during establishment and avoid summer overwatering, which causes rot. Clusters of greenish-white flowers form at the branch tips in spring on mature plants.

planting.note: Plant in spring in gritty, fast-draining soil.

directions: Plant this low, clumping yucca in well-drained soil in full sun, where its thick blue-green leaves edged with curling fibers form a rugged rosette. It is very cold- and drought-tolerant once established. Showy waxy flowers give way to the fleshy, banana-like fruits that give the plant its name.

planting.note: Extremely cold-hardy; plant around the last frost in lean soil.

directions: This Great Plains native forms a low rosette of narrow, blue-green needle leaves and is among the hardiest yuccas, surviving deep cold. Plant in lean, sandy, well-drained soil in full sun and water sparingly. A stalk of greenish-white bell flowers rises in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in deep, fast-draining desert soil.

directions: Plant this trunk-forming desert yucca in deep, sharply drained soil in full sun and water only to establish. Its stiff yellow-green leaves with fibrous margins and a short trunk make a sculptural desert accent. Dense clusters of creamy, purple-tinged flowers appear in spring on established plants.

planting.note: Plant in spring in deep, well-drained ground with room to grow tall.

directions: This large Chihuahuan Desert tree-yucca develops a thick trunk topped with a dense head of broad, rigid leaves. Plant in deep, fast-draining soil in full sun and water sparingly once established. Massive plumes of white flowers held close within the leaf crown appear in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring; tolerates clay better than most yuccas.

directions: This small, trunkless Texas yucca forms a flat rosette of broad, pale blue-green leaves and stays neatly compact. Unlike many yuccas it tolerates heavier soils and part shade, though it prefers full sun and good drainage. A slender stalk of white bell flowers rises in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a hot, dry, well-drained site.

directions: Plant this rugged trunk-forming yucca in deep, fast-draining soil in full sun for a bold desert silhouette. Its long, stiff, yellow-green leaves with shredding fibers persist as a shaggy skirt down the trunk. Dense heads of creamy flowers appear on short stalks in spring.

planting.note: Very hardy variegated selection; plant around the last frost.

directions: This popular selection has soft leaves striped with buttery gold that flushes rosy in cold weather, best grown from divisions to keep the variegation. Plant in well-drained soil in full sun and water only occasionally once established. A tall spike of creamy white flowers rises in summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a lean, fast-draining sunny site.

directions: Formerly classed as a Yucca, this California native forms a tight, spherical rosette of rigid, gray-green, dagger-tipped leaves. Plant in lean, gravelly, fast-draining soil in full sun and water very little. It produces a spectacular tall spike packed with creamy, purple-tinged flowers, then dies after blooming.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a well-drained, sunny bed with room to spread.

directions: This larger relative of red yucca forms a big, upright clump of stiff, fibrous-margined green leaves. Plant in well-drained soil in full sun and water occasionally to establish, then rarely. Very tall stalks of greenish-white flowers rise well above the foliage in summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a sterile selection propagated vegetatively.

directions: This selection is grown for its vivid, true-red flowers on shorter stalks and a longer bloom season than the species. Because it is sterile it sets no seed and pours energy into flowering, so buy nursery divisions rather than sowing. Plant in well-drained soil in full sun and water sparingly for months of hummingbird-friendly bloom.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a well-drained, full-sun location.

directions: This clumping species forms a fountain of very slender, grass-like green leaves with curling white fibers along the edges. Plant in fast-draining soil in full sun and water little once established. Tall wands carry small greenish flowers that open in the evening, giving the plant its name.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, well-drained, sunny spot.

directions: This sotol relative forms a dense, radiating sphere of very long, thin, smooth-edged green leaves atop a slow-forming trunk. Plant in gritty, fast-draining soil in full sun and water sparingly. Mature plants send up a tall flower spike, and unlike agaves the rosette survives blooming.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sharply drained, full-sun bed.

directions: This sotol forms a rounded rosette of narrow, toothed green leaves and slowly develops a short trunk. Plant in fast-draining soil in full sun and water only occasionally once established. A very tall plume-like flower spike rises in summer above the foliage.

planting.note: Plant bare-root canes in spring once the soil has warmed.

directions: Set bare canes upright in gritty, extremely well-drained soil in full sun, planting them at the depth they grew and orienting them as they stood. Mist the stems and water lightly until roots take, then water very sparingly. Established plants leaf out after rains and tip their whip-like canes with brilliant scarlet flower clusters in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a well-drained, sunny location.

directions: This beargrass relative forms a large rosette of stiff, blue-gray, finely toothed leaves and slowly builds a short trunk. Plant in fast-draining soil in full sun and water only to establish. A stout, dense spike of creamy flowers rises well above the foliage on mature plants.

planting.note: Set out rooted cuttings once nights stay reliably above frost.

directions: Root softwood tip cuttings in gritty mix and plant out at the same depth once established. This sprawling, rose-scented shrublet thrives in lean, sharply drained soil and resents wet feet, so water only when the surface dries. Trim lightly after the pale pink flush to keep the mound dense.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings out once soil has warmed after frost.

directions: Take short cuttings and root them in sandy compost before planting at the original level. Named for its small, toothed birch-like leaves, this coastal shrub bears showy pink to mauve flowers with dark veining. It is very drought tolerant and demands sharp drainage, so keep watering sparing and site it in full sun.

planting.note: Plant out after frost in a sharply drained, sheltered bed.

directions: Propagate from stem cuttings and grow in a lean, gravelly mix that dries quickly. The spiny, semi-succulent stems drop their leaves in summer dormancy, then flower in white to pink with a distinctive red heart-shaped mark on the upper petals. Water during the cool growing season and withhold moisture when dormant.

planting.note: Start seed indoors 6 to 8 weeks before setting out after frost.

directions: Sow seed just covered indoors and transplant seedlings after the last frost into free-draining soil. This bushy sub-shrub bears white daisies with deep blue-purple centers and reverse petals that flush blue underneath. Deadhead regularly to prolong bloom, and shear lightly in midsummer if flowering pauses during heat.

planting.note: Plant out after frost in a sunny, free-draining spot.

directions: Grow this cutting-raised cultivar and set plants at the same level as the pot. Its double, pom-like purple flowers stay fully open day and night, unlike single types that close in low light. Provide full sun, consistent watering, and remove spent blooms to sustain flowering.

planting.note: Divide and plant in spring into gritty, sun-baked soil.

directions: Divide clumps in spring and replant the rooted crowns at their former depth. This hardy selection of the trailing African daisy carries deep magenta-pink flowers over spreading, mat-like growth. It needs full sun and excellent drainage, especially in winter, and rewards a light shear after the first flush.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring with the crown just below the surface.

directions: Divide crowded clumps in spring and replant so the fleshy roots sit just under the soil. This evergreen species carries rounded heads of deep blue trumpet flowers on stiff stems above strappy leaves. It flowers best when slightly pot-bound or in firm soil, and appreciates regular summer water tapering off in winter.

planting.note: Set divisions out in spring in full sun and fertile soil.

directions: Split established clumps in spring and replant the divisions at the same depth. This robust evergreen bears large globes of pale to mid-blue flowers in summer and forms broad clumps of arching foliage. Feed and water well during growth for the best flowering, and protect the crowns in the coldest gardens.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in a sunny border with good drainage.

directions: Divide these hardy hybrids in spring and replant at the original depth. Bred for cold tolerance, they are largely deciduous and produce heads of blue to violet flowers in shades varying between seedlings. Grow in full sun with summer moisture and a winter mulch in colder zones for reliable perennial performance.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring; ideal for containers and front edges.

directions: Divide the compact clumps in spring and replant just below the surface. This popular dwarf selection forms low, tidy tufts of foliage topped with sky-blue flower heads on short stems. Its small size suits pots and border fronts; give full sun and steady summer water for repeat flowering.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun with sharp drainage.

directions: Divide crowns in spring and replant at the same level in rich, draining soil. This distinctive selection produces striking bicolor flowers, white at the base grading to deep blue at the petal tips. Grow in full sun with good summer moisture and shelter the crown over winter in marginal climates.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in a warm, sunny, well-drained bed.

directions: Divide the clumps in spring and replant with the crown just below the soil. This free-flowering bicolor bears pendent, tubular flowers that are white at the base and vivid blue at the tips, opening over a long season. Provide full sun and even moisture, and it will rebloom well in containers or borders.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in gritty, moist but drained soil.

directions: Divide the branching, evergreen crowns in spring and replant at the same level. This species forms trunk-like stems clothed in blue-green leaves and bears coral-red spikes that fade to greenish-yellow. It comes from high mountain grassland, so it tolerates cold well provided the soil drains freely in winter.

planting.note: Plant corms in spring in a sunny spot with reliable summer moisture.

directions: Set corms in spring and disturb them as little as possible thereafter. This graceful species arches slender stems that carry pendent, pale pink to mauve bells over evergreen, iris-like leaves. It thrives beside water or in any soil that stays moist in summer yet drains in winter, flowering best in full sun.

planting.note: Plant corms in spring; hardier and more compact than most.

directions: Plant corms in spring in gritty, moisture-retentive soil and leave them undisturbed. This compact, mountain-dwelling species arches shorter stems of coral to dusky-pink bells and is among the hardier wandflowers. Give full sun with summer water and good winter drainage for a graceful, front-of-border display.

planting.note: Plant corms in spring in fertile, moisture-retentive soil.

directions: Plant corms in spring and site them permanently, as they dislike being moved. This selection carries deep wine-purple, almost magenta bells on long, arching stems above evergreen grassy foliage. Keep the soil moist through summer, ensure good winter drainage, and allow the clump time to settle before it flowers strongly.

planting.note: Plant corms in spring in a sunny, sheltered, moist border.

directions: Plant corms in spring and avoid disturbing established clumps. This elegant white-flowered selection dangles pale, silvery bells from arching wands above evergreen foliage. Provide full sun, ample summer moisture, and sharp winter drainage, giving young plants a season or two to reach full flowering.

planting.note: Plant corms in spring in moist, well-drained, sunny ground.

directions: Plant corms in spring in fertile soil that holds summer moisture yet drains in winter. This smaller species carries bright reddish-pink bells on arching stems above narrow evergreen leaves. Leave the clump undisturbed once planted, water well in the growing season, and expect the best flowering after establishment.

planting.note: Plant once frost danger has passed and the soil has warmed.

directions: Set plants into free-draining, low-phosphorus soil at the same depth as the nursery container and mulch lightly around the crown. This vigorous prostrate form roots as it spreads, so allow generous room and prune wayward stems to shape. Toothbrush-shaped red flowers appear from late winter through spring and draw nectar-feeding birds.

planting.note: Plant in spring after the last frost into warm, well-drained ground.

directions: Plant into gritty, phosphorus-free soil in an open sunny spot with good air movement. Water to establish, then taper off as this hardy Robyn Gordon relative is drought tolerant once settled. Large orange-red spider flowers appear almost year-round and are magnets for honeyeaters.

planting.note: Plant in spring into sharply drained soil once frosts have finished.

directions: Choose a hot, well-drained position and plant at container depth with minimal disturbance to the roots. Grey-green narrow foliage sets off clusters of pink to red spider flowers through winter and spring. Trim lightly after flowering to keep the bush dense and avoid feeding with phosphorus-rich fertilizer.

planting.note: Plant in spring into light, well-drained soil after frost.

directions: Plant this open, wiry shrub in sandy or gravelly soil where drainage is fast. Delicate pink spider-flower clusters open through much of the year, with a strong flush in winter and spring. Keep it lean, avoid phosphorus, and tip-prune to encourage a tidier habit.

planting.note: Plant in spring into warm, well-drained soil once frost risk passes.

directions: Give this large, fast-growing banksii-type grevillea room and plant it at nursery depth in free-draining ground. Big pink cylindrical flower heads appear through most of the year and feed nectar birds heavily. Water while establishing, then rely on rainfall, and prune after main flushes to control size.

planting.note: Plant in spring into well-drained soil after the last frost.

directions: Plant into open, sunny, sharply drained soil and mulch away from the stem. This hardy hybrid carries pink-and-cream spider flowers over a long season and tolerates coastal exposure. Establish with regular water, then reduce watering, and lightly prune spent flowers to keep it bushy.

planting.note: Plant in spring into free-draining soil once frost has finished.

directions: Set this olive-leaved shrub into gritty, well-drained soil in full sun and water in well. Curved spider flowers in orange, red, or yellow crowd the branch tips from winter into spring. It handles dry, coastal and limy conditions once established and responds well to a trim after flowering.

planting.note: Plant in spring into sandy, well-drained soil after frost.

directions: Plant this fine-leaved, spreading shrub in a sunny, sharply drained spot at container depth. Fern-like grey-green foliage carries red spider flowers over a long season, peaking in spring. Water to establish then keep dry, avoid phosphorus, and tip-prune to maintain a rounded shape.

planting.note: Plant in spring into well-drained soil once hard frosts ease.

directions: Plant this holly-leaved, suckering shrub in free-draining soil in full sun to light shade. The prickly blue-green foliage sets off toothbrush-shaped red-and-cream flowers through spring and summer. It is frost tolerant and drought hardy once established; prune to shape after the main bloom.

planting.note: Plant in spring into well-drained soil; this hybrid tolerates light frost.

directions: Plant this dense, frost-hardy rosmarinifolia hybrid in full sun and sharp drainage at nursery depth. Deep red spider flowers appear over a very long season, mainly through winter and spring, feeding small birds. Water in well, then treat as drought tolerant, and clip after flowering to keep it compact.

planting.note: Plant in spring into moist, well-drained soil after frost.

directions: Plant this rigid, upright bottlebrush in full sun in soil that stays moist but drains freely. Bright scarlet brushes crowd the stems in spring and often again in autumn, attracting honeyeaters. Water regularly while young and prune just below the spent flowers to promote bushy new growth.

planting.note: Plant in late spring into warm soil; this species dislikes frost.

directions: Give this tropical bottlebrush a warm, sheltered, sunny position in fertile, well-drained soil. Red brushes tipped with golden anthers appear mainly in spring, and bronze new growth adds seasonal color. Keep the soil evenly moist while establishing and prune after flowering to shape.

planting.note: Plant in spring into moist soil; this species tolerates light frost.

directions: Plant this compact, fine-leaved bottlebrush in a sunny spot in soil that holds some moisture. Small crimson brushes appear through summer and it copes with damp ground and occasional flooding. Water regularly while young, then trim after flowering to keep the low mound neat.

planting.note: Plant in spring into moist, acidic soil after frost.

directions: Plant this thick-leaved coastal bottlebrush in a sunny, damp position in sandy, acidic soil. Red or occasionally green brushes appear in spring and summer and tolerate periodically wet feet. Keep the root zone moist, mulch well, and prune lightly after each flush.

planting.note: Plant in spring into well-drained soil once frost has passed.

directions: Plant this upright cultivar in full sun in soil that stays moist yet drains well. Soft pink bottlebrush flowers appear mainly in spring and again in autumn, drawing birds and bees. Water while establishing, then prune the stem tips after flowering to build a dense, well-branched bush.

planting.note: Plant in spring into well-drained soil; this species tolerates dry, frosty sites.

directions: Plant this hardy inland bottlebrush in a hot, sunny, well-drained position where it can dry out between waterings. Fine, sharp foliage carries red brushes tipped with gold anthers through summer. It is notably drought and frost tolerant; prune after flowering to keep it compact.

planting.note: Plant in late spring into warm, well-drained soil; frost sensitive when young.

directions: Plant this graceful weeping tea tree in a warm, sheltered position in free-draining soil. Fine pendulous foliage and papery bark give year-round appeal, with small white flowers appearing in the warmer months. Water regularly while establishing and shelter from hard frost.

planting.note: Plant in spring into sandy, well-drained soil; tolerates light frost.

directions: Plant this small heathland tea tree in sandy, sharply drained soil in full sun to part shade. White to pale pink flowers smother the wiry branches in spring. It suits low-water native gardens; water to establish and trim lightly after flowering to keep it bushy.

planting.note: Plant in spring into well-drained sandy soil after frost.

directions: Plant this open shrub in sandy, free-draining soil in full sun to dappled light. Unusually, the pink flowers open along the older wood rather than the tips, appearing mostly from summer into autumn. Water to establish, keep it lean, and prune after flowering to encourage branching.

planting.note: Plant in spring into well-drained soil after the last frost.

directions: Plant this spreading, low correa in dappled shade or morning sun in free-draining soil enriched with a little organic matter. Dusky pink-red tubular bells hang from the branches through autumn and winter, feeding small birds. Water to establish, then keep moderately dry, and trim after flowering to maintain density.

planting.note: Plant in spring into well-drained soil; tolerates light frost.

directions: Plant this glossy-leaved correa in well-drained soil in part shade or full sun in cooler areas. Greenish to yellow-green tubular bells appear from autumn through winter and the crushed foliage is aromatic. It is hardy and drought tolerant once established; clip lightly to shape.

planting.note: Plant in spring into free-draining soil after frost.

directions: Plant this dainty, spreading correa in light shade or morning sun in sharply drained soil. Slender salmon-pink to orange bells hang along the arching stems through autumn and winter. Water to settle it in, then keep on the dry side, and give a light trim after flowering.

planting.note: Plant in spring into well-drained soil; tolerant of coastal wind and light frost.

directions: Plant this tough, coastal correa in well-drained soil in sun or part shade where it can screen wind and salt spray. Furry, rounded leaves back cream to pale green tubular bells through autumn and winter. Water to establish, then treat as drought and salt tolerant, and shear lightly to keep it dense.

planting.note: Plant in spring into well-drained soil after frost.

directions: Plant this popular hybrid in part shade or morning sun in free-draining soil with light mulch. Long two-toned bells of pink shading to yellow-green hang along the stems through autumn and winter, attracting nectar birds. Water to establish, avoid phosphorus, and trim after flowering to shape.

planting.note: Plant in spring into moist, well-drained soil; tolerates frost and shade.

directions: Plant this tall, shade-loving correa in cool, moist, well-drained soil under a canopy or on a sheltered south side. Narrow greenish-yellow bells appear through the cooler months and suit woodland-style gardens. Keep the root zone moist and mulched, and prune to control the upright, sometimes tree-like form.

planting.note: Plant in spring into moist, well-drained soil after frost.

directions: Plant this distinctive correa in part shade in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil. Yellow-green bells are topped by a flared, chef's-hat-like calyx and appear mainly through autumn and winter. Keep it evenly moist and sheltered from hot afternoon sun, trimming lightly to maintain shape.

planting.note: Plant in spring into sharply drained soil after frost.

directions: Plant this tall flavidus-type paw in full sun in very free-draining soil, keeping the crown at soil level. Rich red flowers rise on strong stems through spring and summer and make excellent long-lasting cut flowers. Water moderately in the growing season, and cut spent flower stems and old foliage back to the base to refresh the clump.

planting.note: Plant in spring into well-drained soil after frost.

directions: Plant this compact dwarf paw in full sun in gritty, well-drained soil, spacing plants for good airflow. Bright red flowers appear over a long season from spring into autumn, ideal for pots and borders. Water sparingly, remove finished stems, and divide congested clumps every few years to keep them vigorous.

planting.note: Plant in spring into sandy, well-drained soil after frost.

directions: Plant this low-growing paw in full sun in sharply drained sandy soil, keeping it dry in summer dormancy. Woolly flowers in shades of red, orange and yellow appear on short stems in spring. Water lightly during active growth and allow the clump to rest through the hottest months.

planting.note: Plant in spring into very free-draining soil after frost.

directions: Plant this tall paw in full sun in sandy, sharply drained soil and keep it lean and dry. Golden-yellow woolly flowers top branching stems in spring and early summer and last well when cut. Water moderately during growth, cut spent stems to the base, and avoid soggy soil that promotes ink-spot disease.

planting.note: Plant in spring into well-drained soil after frost.

directions: Plant this compact, long-flowering paw in full sun in gritty, free-draining soil. Soft pink flowers appear over many months from spring through autumn, making it a reliable container and border plant. Water sparingly, remove old flower stems and foliage, and divide crowded clumps to maintain flowering.

planting.note: Plant in spring into moist, well-drained soil after frost.

directions: Plant this rounded hebe in full sun to light shade in fertile, moist but well-drained soil. Long tapering spikes of pink flowers fading to white appear from summer into autumn, attracting bees and butterflies. Water in dry spells, mulch the roots, and trim after flowering to keep the bush neat.

planting.note: Plant in spring into well-drained soil; tolerates light frost.

directions: Plant this compact hebe in full sun or part shade in well-drained soil enriched with organic matter. Short violet-purple flower spikes appear over a long season from summer well into autumn, set against dark green foliage. Water during dry weather and clip lightly after the main flush to maintain a rounded shape.

planting.note: Plant in spring into well-drained soil after frost; tolerant of coastal sites.

directions: Plant this glossy-leaved coastal hebe in full sun in well-drained soil where it can shrug off salt wind. Bold reddish-purple flower spikes appear through summer and autumn against thick, deep green leaves. Water moderately, mulch the roots, and trim spent flowers to encourage repeat blooming.

planting.note: Plant in spring into well-drained soil; hardy to light frost.

directions: Plant this low, spreading hebe in full sun in gritty, well-drained soil as an edging or groundcover. Blue-grey foliage forms a dense mat studded with small white flowers in late spring and early summer. It is hardy and low-maintenance; water to establish and shear back if it becomes untidy.

planting.note: Plant in spring into moist, well-drained soil after frost.

directions: Plant this rounded Wiri-series hebe in full sun to part shade in fertile, well-drained soil. Bright rose-purple flower spikes crowd the bush through summer, drawing bees and butterflies. Water during dry periods, mulch the base, and trim after flowering to keep it compact and free-blooming.

planting.note: Plant in spring into well-drained soil; hardy and tolerant of frost.

directions: Plant this whipcord hebe in full sun in free-draining soil where its conifer-like form stands out. Tiny scale leaves clothe the branchlets, and small pale blue to lilac flowers appear in early summer. It is hardy and drought tolerant once established; prune lightly to keep the tidy dome shape.

planting.note: Lift and split established clumps in spring and replant the divisions promptly.

directions: Set divisions at the depth they grew previously in gritty, well-drained soil. Prized for oversized felted leaves and few flowers, it works best as a foliage groundcover in dry beds and edges. Comb out any matted or blackened leaves after wet spells so the crown stays dry.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring so they root before summer heat arrives.

directions: Space the low rosettes so they knit into an even silver mat over lean, sharply drained ground. This selection rarely produces flower stalks, so all the effort goes into dense foliage that stays neat with little care. Trim away winter-damaged leaves in early spring to refresh the planting.

planting.note: Sow indoors in late winter or set plants out after the last hard frost.

directions: Press seed onto the surface and keep it cool and moist during germination. Grow the basal rosettes in average, well-drained soil where they form tidy clumps that tolerate some drought once established. Upright spikes of reddish-purple flowers open through summer and can be deadheaded to keep the display fresh.

planting.note: Divide and replant clumps in spring for reliable, uniform stands.

directions: Set divisions at their original depth in fertile, well-drained soil and water them in well. This award-winning selection throws dense, even spikes of rosy-violet flowers over glossy basal foliage in midsummer. Shear the spent stems to tidy the clump, which spreads slowly into a dependable drift.

planting.note: Sow indoors before the last frost and set out once nights stay warm.

directions: Barely cover seed and germinate warm; seedlings grow quickly into bushy plants. Give them sun to light shade and well-drained soil in a spot sheltered from cold winds. Spikes of tubular scarlet flowers draw hummingbirds from late spring until frost, so keep deadheading for continuous bloom.

planting.note: Root cuttings or plant nursery stock in spring into sharply drained soil.

directions: Set plants in lean, gritty soil where the silver-gray filigree foliage will stay dense and aromatic. Button-like yellow flowers dot the mound in summer, though many gardeners shear them off to keep a formal silver form. Cut the whole plant back hard in spring to prevent it from going woody and open in the center.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring for tight, low edging.

directions: Use this compact form for low knot-garden hedging in poor, fast-draining soil. Its fine silver foliage stays naturally dense, and light yellow buttons appear in summer above the mound. Trim after flowering and again in spring to hold the neat cushion shape.

planting.note: Set cuttings or plants out in spring into dry, sunny ground.

directions: Plant in thin, well-drained soil where the bright green, thread-fine foliage releases a resinous scent when brushed. Bright yellow button flowers cover the plant in summer and are attractive to bees. Shear back after bloom and prune in spring to keep the bush compact and floriferous.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in full sun and sharp drainage.

directions: Grow this selection in lean, gritty soil for a mound of green feathery foliage. It is valued for its softer, pale primrose-yellow flowers that are less harsh than the type in summer. Clip after flowering to keep the plant tidy and cut back in spring before new growth breaks.

planting.note: Plant cuttings in spring into free-draining, sunny soil.

directions: Give this larger cotton lavender room in poor, dry soil where its long, feathery silver foliage can arch gracefully. Lemon-yellow flower buttons rise well above the leaves on slender stalks in summer. Prune hard in spring to keep the plant from splaying open with age.

planting.note: Plant in spring into gritty, sharply drained soil in full sun.

directions: Grow this gray-green woolly subshrub in dry, poor soil where excellent drainage keeps the felted foliage healthy. It is grown mainly for its rounded silvery mound and the curious green calyx cups that hold small pinkish-white flowers in summer. Cut back in spring to renew the foliage and prevent legginess.

planting.note: Set out in spring into hot, dry, well-drained ground.

directions: Plant in lean, sharply drained soil in the hottest, driest spot available. The rounded gray felted leaves and distinctive saucer-shaped calyces make it a striking dry-garden plant, with small purplish flowers in summer. The seed heads dry well, and a hard spring prune keeps the plant compact.

planting.note: Plant in spring into fast-draining soil so roots establish before summer heat.

directions: Set this vigorous hybrid in poor, sharply drained soil and avoid rich feeding that softens growth. Large, crumpled pure-white flowers open across the bush in early summer, each lasting a single day but produced in generous succession. Prune only lightly after flowering, since old wood resents hard cutting.

planting.note: Plant in spring into hot, dry, gritty soil in full sun.

directions: Give this compact species the leanest, driest position you have, as it thrives in near-drought once rooted. Small clusters of white flowers appear over rosemary-like foliage in late spring and early summer. Trim lightly after bloom to shape it, but never cut back into leafless old wood.

planting.note: Plant in spring once frost has passed into warm, well-drained soil.

directions: This tender rockrose needs a frost-free, sunny site with sharp drainage or a large container that can be sheltered. Rose-pink flowers with a yellow center open over sticky, veined green leaves through spring and summer. Water sparingly and shape lightly after flowering, avoiding cuts into bare wood.

planting.note: Plant in spring into free-draining soil in a warm, sunny spot.

directions: Plant this reliable hybrid in poor, gritty soil and keep feeding to a minimum. Soft gray-green foliage sets off a long summer succession of clear pink single flowers. Give it a sheltered position in colder gardens and prune only lightly to shape after the main flush.

planting.note: Plant in spring on a sunny bank or wall top with sharp drainage.

directions: Use this low, wide-spreading selection to cover dry banks or spill over walls in full sun. White flowers marked with a crimson basal blotch open in early summer above dark green foliage. Plant in lean soil, water sparingly once established, and trim lightly after flowering.

planting.note: Plant in spring into hot, well-drained ground in full sun.

directions: Grow this graceful hybrid in poor, gritty soil where it will flower freely without coddling. Wavy-edged pale pink petals open around a yellow center over narrow, wrinkled green leaves in early summer. Keep pruning light and never cut into old bare wood, which will not resprout.

planting.note: Plant in spring into sharply drained soil in a warm, sunny site.

directions: This softly woolly subshrub wants lean, fast-draining soil and a spot sheltered from cold, wet winds. Whorls of pale lilac-pink hooded flowers rise above the gray-green felted leaves in early summer. Trim after flowering to keep the plant bushy and prevent it from becoming leggy.

planting.note: Plant in spring into dry, well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: Give this larger species room in poor, sharply drained soil and full sun. Silvery-gray woolly foliage forms a rounded shrub topped with whorls of dusky pink-purple flowers in late spring. Prune after flowering to control size, cutting back to healthy leafy growth rather than bare stems.

planting.note: Plant in spring into gritty, free-draining soil in full sun.

directions: This tidy, low subshrub suits small dry gardens and gravel plantings in poor soil. Rounded, densely felted green-gray leaves form a compact mound topped by whorls of golden-yellow hooded flowers in summer. Clip lightly after bloom to maintain its neat, dome-shaped habit.

planting.note: Plant in spring into well-drained soil in full sun for best leaf color.

directions: Plant in lean, sharply drained soil and full sun, where the foliage takes on its distinctive golden-green cast, especially in summer. Golden-yellow flowers appear in whorls above the felted leaves in early summer. Prune lightly after flowering to keep the shrub dense and to encourage fresh, well-colored growth.

planting.note: Start indoors before the last frost or plant out in spring.

directions: Unlike the shrubby species, this hardy herbaceous perennial dies back each winter and returns from the crown. Grow it in well-drained soil in full sun where sturdy stems carry tiered whorls of soft lilac-pink flowers in early summer. Cut the stems down after bloom or leave the seed whorls for winter structure.

planting.note: Plant in spring into free-draining soil in a warm, sunny spot.

directions: This vigorous hybrid forms a large, softly gray-green mound and needs room and sharp drainage. Big whorls of pale primrose-yellow hooded flowers stand well above the felted foliage in summer. Prune after flowering to keep it shapely and to stop the base from becoming bare and woody.

planting.note: Start indoors before the last frost or set out in spring.

directions: Barely cover the seed and germinate in warmth; plants grow quickly into leafy clumps. Give them well-drained soil in sun to light shade, where slender spikes of reddish-purple flowers rise from midsummer into fall. Deadhead to prolong bloom, though it self-sows gently where happy.

planting.note: Plant in spring into very sharply drained, gritty soil in full sun.

directions: This low, spreading subshrub demands lean, gritty soil and full sun to thrive, and rots in damp conditions. Woolly white stems and leaves form a silvery cushion topped with small heads of creamy-white to pale pink flowers in summer. Trim lightly after flowering to keep the cushion neat.

planting.note: Plant in spring into gritty, alkaline, sharply drained soil in full sun.

directions: A tough alpine for rock gardens and troughs, it needs gritty, free-draining, preferably limy soil and full sun. The low evergreen mat carries narrow gray-green leaves and rounded clusters of creamy-yellow flowers in early summer. Give it a lean diet and sharp drainage, and shear lightly after bloom.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring into fast-draining soil in full sun.

directions: Use this low, wide-spreading germander as a fragrant groundcover or trailing edge in dry, sunny sites. Fine silvery foliage carries a long summer show of rosy-lilac flowers that draw bees and butterflies. Plant in sharply drained soil, water sparingly, and shear after the main flush to renew growth.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring, spacing closely for a low clipped hedge.

directions: This robust hybrid is a favorite for low clipped hedging and knot gardens, tolerating shearing better than the true wall germander. Set young plants closely in well-drained soil and full sun to knit into a dense edge. Glossy evergreen leaves carry short spikes of rosy-pink flowers in summer; clip after bloom to keep crisp lines.

planting.note: Plant in spring into well-drained soil in full sun to light shade.

directions: Grow this cream-variegated selection in free-draining soil, giving it room to form an upright evergreen dome. Domed heads of lime-green flowers with creamy edges top the whitish-margined foliage in spring. Wear gloves and cut spent flowering stems to the base after bloom, as the milky sap is a skin and eye irritant.

planting.note: Plant in spring into sharply drained soil in sun or part shade.

directions: Set this striking variegated spurge in gritty, well-drained soil where its gray-green, white-edged leaves brighten the border. Large heads of green-and-cream flowers appear in spring on upright evergreen stems. Cut each flowered stem to ground level after blooming, and handle carefully because the sap irritates skin and eyes.

planting.note: Plant in spring into well-drained soil in full sun for strongest color.

directions: Plant this compact hybrid in free-draining soil and full sun, where the cream-edged foliage flushes pink and red in cold weather. Lime-green and yellow flowers with a red eye appear over a long spring season. Shear off spent flowering stems to the base, protecting your hands from the irritant white sap.

planting.note: Plant in spring into gritty, sharply drained soil in full sun.

directions: This drought-tough spurge wants poor, gritty soil and full sun, where its sprawling stems of blue-gray succulent leaves make a bold form. Large chartreuse-yellow flower heads open at the stem tips in late winter and spring, aging to orange-red. Cut flowered stems to the base afterward and handle with gloves, as the sap irritates skin.

planting.note: Plant out after the last frost once nights are reliably warm.

directions: Grown as an annual in most climates, this sterile hybrid makes an airy cloud of tiny white bracts from planting until frost. Set plants out after danger of frost in well-drained soil or containers, in full sun to light shade. It needs no deadheading and blooms nonstop; wear gloves when trimming, as the milky sap can irritate skin.
